Yeah, this is right.

For a while, there were the big house tete de cuvees, then Selosse (and I guess Salon), then the mass of growers, where no one had really distanced themselves in terms of pricing. There was always the question of which grower the “next Selosse” was going to be in terms of pricing. That question now seems to be answered, with Collin, Bouchard, and a small handful of others.

(NB: I think we’re seeing a bit of the same dynamic in Germany, where you have Keller, which like Selosse has really distanced itself from the pack pricing-wise. The question is, who’s next?)

The prices on Collin haven’t gone up they much, actually. However, he had been quite clear that he wants a lot of his champagne to be drunk at good restaurants.

What does that have to do with listing wines in wine-searcher? Places like EMP have the mags of all the really hard to find stuff because he wants it there. Some retailer putting his wines on wine-searcher won’t change that. Come on.

I was responding to why there are a lot fewer bottles available these days. He doesn’t allow them on WS because he’s extremely conscious of the distribution of his wines. It’s not about raising scarcity to raise prices - the prices on the wines haven’t massively increased.

Collin has been my favorite champagne producer for years, I’m following the pricing closely by buying lots of it for years.

If people can’t find it on WS, they assume it’s sold out. Then certain retailers will post it at higher pricing cause it’s not available on searches. Which then people who can’t find them online for prior pricing end up buying from these places and after a bit the pricing becomes the new normal as each new release the retailers that aren’t posting it online but offering it to their customers, start to inch their pricing up to get closer to WS low.

Tale as old as time for quite a few producers. When most people don’t know what the “release” pricing is on the wine, it tends to escalate the price in the nearish future.

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
Olivier’s sales and marketing decisions, especially the decision to focus heavily on restaurant sales over private clients goes more towards who he wants to drink his wines and in what setting rather than an attempt to manufacture scarcity. If he wanted to raise prices, a much more efficient method would be to hold more wine back for later release.

Rather, as I see it, the ‘manipulation’ seems to be from retailer trying to extract as much money as possible from a rare wine. To that end, retailers do it all the time, to varying degrees with wines from all corners of the world. Pick a highly allocated wine, and one is bound to find a retailer listed on wine-searcher listing it for a lot more than other places, or what others have recently paid.

It is also worth mentioning that prices listed on wine-searcher for rare wines aren’t often the market or market clearing price. Rather, these very high prices outside of the normal range of what most have paid for the bottle is often a retailer’s attempt at overreaching a price for a rare bottle. The result is that it often sits on wine-searcher for a long time until someone with a lot money decides they want it regardless of cost. They forgo high velocity for a high profit. Sometimes it works well, other times they can be stuck holding inventory for a while until they find someone willing to pay the exorbitant amount.

Collin is a producer of a good. He has a choice to whom to sell the good. He has chosen to ask his distributors to sell that good to restaurants (who can mark it up as they choose) as opposed to retailers (who can also mark it up as they choose). The price that is charged for his goods to the end user isn’t actually set by Collin and he doesn’t receive a portion of that price. So when you say he’s “manipulating the market”, what is he manipulating? The choice of whom to sell? Sure, in the same way as every single producer makes a choice. I also choose to whom to sell my services, that does not mean I am manipulating the market for my services.

The implication to “it’s manipulation” is that he is increasing the price paid by consumers for a specific reason. I am asking, what is the reason? Because there appears to be no benefit to him from that increase in price. If your argument is that his actions have caused the wines to become more expensive for consumers - yes, that appears to be true (though how much of that is due to this policy and how much of that is due to the high scores isn’t knowable). But that does not make it manipulation. It just means he has made a choice that has increase the price of the wine in the secondary market. Those things are not the same.

To draw an obvious parallel, DRC made a Petit Monts in 2018, the first time they have done so. They only sold that cuvee to restaurants. I am certain the wine can be purchased in the secondary market and I am certain it is very expensive. I have not yet seen anyone argue this is “market manipulation” by DRC because they have made this cuvee very hard to find and buy.

But that does not make it manipulation. It just means he has made a choice that has increase the price of the wine in the secondary market. Those things are not the same.

Since we are just speculating (and that’s all I’m doing here), it could be so that he can raise the prices in the future.

With a Napa Cult Cab, another category where perceived scarcity helps drive up the hype and mystique around the wine, the wines will sell for a high markup at auction and retail in the early years, and then gradually the winery bumps its release prices up 10-15% a year until the release price captures all or most of the market value.

Hypothetically, by manipulating the market to create the impression of a bidding war over his products and making them really expensive to consumers, he will be able to realize higher prices steadily more and more in the years to come.

Of course we don’t know if this is right but it’s what I came to suggest as well. What is the end goal of only being in the best restaurants around the biggest cities?

If I were a producer I’d be a bit miffed at a retailer charging multiples of what it should retail for as well. That said I’m not sure restaurants are the general solution to that specific problem.

I think it is two separate issues

The focus on restaurant sales, as I understand it, is in large part due to Olivier’s desire that his wines be drunk with great food to accompany it.

His requests that retailers not list the wine online seem to be an attempt at limiting price increases and a few people buying up all the inventory. I think part of the idea was that you’d have to have a relationship with a retailer to get to the wines, so one couldn’t simply go on wine-searcher and clear out everything they see at attractive prices.

Whether or not one agrees with those decisions or believe those are effective methods to achieving Olivier’s desired results are a different matter.

posts.name: Rick Allen
posts.cooked:
There are a bunch of wines that end up being restaurant only. There are a couple of reasons why. First, restaurants are very good customers of distributors, so if there is a limited supply, it’s not surprising that the restaurants get first crack. Second, selling to restaurants means that more people will get a chance to try your wines. Certainly selling a couple cases to a restaurant likely means that at least 50 people will drink the wine versus a much smaller number if the wine is sold to an individual.

We actually take the same approach with our beer. I would much rather sell a keg to a restaurant than to somebody with a home kegerator. Our product is very limited, so let’s give more people a chance to enjoy it.

I’ve heard of a wine or two being restaurant-only. Klaus Peter Keller did a restaurant only version of his schubertslay kabi. Of course, there was another schubertslay kabi that anyone willing to pay the tariff could buy, and tons of other keller wines available to the average consumer. What else is restaurant only?

There are tons of wines that are “on premise”. Every mailing list Napa brand that you can buy in restaurants has distribution but the producer wants the wines exclusively in restaurants (“on premise”) as opposed to retail (“off premise”). The thinking behind this is that it theoretically reaches more people by spreading it around to restaurants (folks are unlikely to drink a 6-pack of something in a sitting) and it also supports the mailing list pricing. Retail is hyper-competitive and low margin and completely out of the control of the producer and they worry that it could damage the perceived value of the brand to their mailing list customers. This is a fairly new tactic for a European producer (but the big houses practice a form of this, too) IME, but Olivier’s operation has more in common with a cult cab than with established wine brands. It’s a relatively new operation in a pricey neighborhood that has really gained some traction. I’ll be interested to see how this works out. He’s very serious about it. Word was passed through the importer to local distributors that if bottles showed up on “the internet” at retail, that would be the last bottles they’d see.

I don’t think one can explain what happened to Ulysse Collin in saying that he wants his champagnes solely sold in restaurants. When Olivier Collin received such high points from the wine advocate, he was delighted and finally felt he had received the regocniton he deserved. I think he was totally niave however to the consequences of high points.

Wine investment firms listed his champagne as one of the few grower champagnes to invest in, which really pushed the secondary prices up.

We lost our allocation because a customer put a bottle up for sale on italy’s EBAY. Collin had no understanding for the fact, once we sell a bottle, we have no control over its destiny. Many other cavistes, who supported him from the beginning lost allocations for more and more crazy reasons.

It is not that he does not only want his champagnes shown on the internet, he does not want them displayed in shops. It is said, he visited a 3 star restaurant with a natural wine disposition and was so furious not to find his champagne on the list. He called the importer from the restaurant before eating and threatened to cancel that countries allocation, if the importer did not get his champagne on the list. I don’t know if the importer managed to do this.

What is perhaps more interesting is recent developments in the Champagne. Selosse received 100 points for a 2008 champagne, the price went up to 6000 €. Selosse was furious, to the point that he threatened to retire to a monastery. I believe now they want to number or track the bottles to prevent such price jumps. Good luck.

What is perhaps even more interesting is how will the markets accept Egly-Ouriets 2013 and the late degorged 2008. The 13 received a timely 100 points from the wine advocate. The ex-cellar price has been adjusted accordingly, I estimate the 2013 will sell between 500 € and 600 € and the 2008 from 600 € +. It seems, well it is a well known fact, Egly is cleverer than Selosse and UC and wants his piece of the cake.

As someone who sells and specialises in grower champagne, we get asked a lot, who are the next big names. I guess only William Kelley, knows that as the supposed kingmaker : of champagne.

Georges Remy without the help of a tester, is reaching cult status. Jacques Lassaigne similiarly so.
Domaine Vincey off to a flying start. La Borderie also have the potential to step into UC shoes.

The saddest thing perhaps, is the Chiquet brothers stopping, this being their last harvest. A french telephone firm will apparently take over, and a chef de cave from the Krug stable will take over the winemaking. One can just hope the prices do not explode.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Jon_Lawrence:

So I had dinner at Sunday Vinyl last night, which is the across from the breezeway from Tavernetta and part of the Bobby Stuckey food empire. It was a great experience that I will write up separately, but the somm used to work at Tavernetta and she described the ‘15 Jardin as a bit more oxidative and would work better with an autumnal dish. The Roises is fresher, creamier, stone fruit driven, better with oysters. Knowing my palate, I am leaning towards the Roises. I am going to have lunch at Tavernetta on Thursday so I will finalize the choices for our Friday dinner with the somm then.

I think the somm is somewhat conflating “oxidative” with “more oak influence” which people sometimes do with Champagne (Krug is an obvious example). Some old Collins were definitely more oxidative (especially the Maillons), but the Jardin isn’t really an oxidative wine, but rather shows more of the typical “ginger” note that Collin wines often have and which is more of the oak than oxidation. I think the Roises (which is generally my favorite cuvee) has a more slightly blue driven fruit profile which sometimes almost reminds me of some GG rieslings (though it’s a faint reminder!). I don’t think of any of the current Collin releases as “creamy” though - that’s how the older wines were made; I opened an 08/11 disgorgement PIerrieres last month and that is a creamy wine.

Either way, you can’t go wrong with those cuvees - enjoy!

posts.name: John Vizuete
posts.cooked:
Sure, there’s no hurry, obviously. Here’s the last note on the 2017 from CT, and having had a few of these, I can see where he is coming from:

dbkitc wrote:

94 points

10/15/2022 “Deep golden color. Seems mature for age. The nose is filled with toasted brioche, chalk and white grapes. Mature fruit. Deep and winey. There is a red fruit note on the finish that really shines. A very mature rendering of this wine and I like it but glad I drank it tonight. Buttered toast with white grape jam. Yum”

To my palate, the wine is structured and plenty acidic, and newer renditions are perhaps better than the first few vintages, so of course I expect it to age fine. But if I were in your shoes I can’t imagine buying a wine I’ve been wanting to taste and then sitting on it for 5 years!

As for the comparison to 2008, I’ll just say that plenty of the 08s are quite incisive and linear, and should age quite slowly, so it’s not entirely mad
